refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote operators must reliably command and receive feedback from equipment. The 0.999 threshold (approximately 43 minutes downtime per month) is a standard for safety-critical teleoperation systems. Refinement clarifies measurement scope (bidirectional link), evaluation period (customer-defined), and the quantitative target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; automated logging of outage duration and root cause; statistical analysis of availability = (total_time - total_downtime) / total_time.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss and report status to Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to minimize link downtime.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication path with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover and health monitoring
DP-C: Multi-path routing with load balancing and dynamic path selection based on link quality metrics
DP-D: Hybrid approach combining dual paths with quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and predictive link health management
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover and health monitoring. Primary path uses primary network infrastructure; backup path uses independent network infrastructure. Automatic failover triggered by primary path health degradation (latency, packet loss, or timeout). Continuous health monitoring on both paths reports status via SafetyAlert interface.

potential_sources: Coupling between link availability and vehicle mobility (vehicle must be capable of safe operation during link transitions)
Coupling between failover logic and Safety Services (safety state must be maintained during switchover)
Coupling between network infrastructure selection and cost/deployment constraints (not addressed at this level)
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is decoupled from specific network technologies (cellular, satellite, mesh) and from vehicle control logic. Failover is transparent to the operator. Safety Services receives status updates but does not control failover logic, maintaining separation of concerns.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent network paths are available at the worksite (customer responsibility).
Assumption: Failover latency (< 100 ms) is acceptable for safe teleoperation.
Risk: If both paths fail simultaneously, link availability target cannot be met; mitigation requires redundancy at network provider level (out of scope).
Risk: Backup path activation may introduce transient operator experience degradation; mitigation requires operator training and UI feedback.
Risk: Health monitoring overhead may consume bandwidth; mitigation requires efficient heartbeat protocol design.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify failover latency requirement and acceptable operator experience during switchover. Define health monitoring frequency and thresholds (e.g., 3 consecutive missed heartbeats = failover trigger). Specify network path independence criteria (e.g., geographically diverse, different providers).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-path communication architecture, failover logic, health monitoring, and link status reporting. Includes command transmission, telemetry reception, path selection, and SafetyAlert interface. Does not own vehicle control logic, Safety Services decision-making, or network infrastructure provisioning.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from tele-operator via primary or backup path.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, operational status) to tele-operator via primary or backup path.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports link health status (primary path up/down, backup path up/down, failover events, latency/packet loss metrics) to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts underlying network infrastructure (primary and backup paths); Teleoperation module interfaces with Network Link to send/receive packets without knowledge of specific technology (cellular, satellite, etc.).

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of dual-path logic, failover, and health monitoring. All core DP-B functions are contained. Minor diffusion: Network Link interface abstraction requires coordination with network provisioning (outside Teleoperation scope).

Vehicle should not own teleoperation link logic; it is a consumer of Tasking commands and producer of Telemetry. Placing failover logic in Vehicle would couple vehicle control to network management, violating separation of concerns.

Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert status but should not own failover or health monitoring logic. Safety Services role is to react to link status (e.g., trigger safe shutdown if link unavailable), not to manage the link itself.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own detailed teleoperation link logic. Assigning to NGHE Solution would defer architectural clarity to lower levels.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns the dual-path communication architecture, failover state machine, health monitoring timers, and SafetyAlert reporting. Vehicle module consumes Tasking and produces Telemetry without awareness of link redundancy. Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert and implements safe-state logic (e.g., emergency stop if link unavailable for > threshold). Network Link interface abstracts physical network paths.
containment_risks: Risk: If network infrastructure provisioning (e.g., cellular modem configuration, satellite link setup) is not clearly owned, Teleoperation may become responsible for tasks outside its scope.
Risk: If Vehicle module is not disciplined to ignore link status and only consume Tasking/produce Telemetry, coupling will diffuse failover logic into Vehicle.
Risk: If SafetyAlert interface is not well-defined, Safety Services may attempt to control failover, creating bidirectional coupling.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define Network Link interface contract (what Teleoperation expects from network layer). Document that Vehicle module is link-agnostic and only responds to Tasking commands. Clarify that SafetyAlert is unidirectional (Teleoperation → Safety Services) and Safety Services does not command failover.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual independent paths with automatic failover and health monitoring) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥ 0.999 link availability). Dual paths provide redundancy to meet the 99.9% target. Automatic failover minimizes downtime during path transitions. Health monitoring enables proactive detection and reporting. No independence violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: Network infrastructure provisioning (e.g., selecting and configuring primary/backup network providers) is not owned by Teleoperation; this must be a separate concern (likely owned by System Engineer or Project Manager). Teleoperation assumes Network Link interface is available.
Diffusion risk: Vehicle module must remain disciplined to not implement link-aware logic; all failover and health monitoring must stay in Teleoperation.
Diffusion risk: SafetyAlert interface must be unidirectional; if Safety Services attempts to command failover, coupling will increase.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module cleanly contains the DP-B logic (dual paths, failover state machine, health monitoring). External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not leak implementation details. Ownership boundary is clear.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation by simulating primary/backup path failures and measuring failover latency and availability metrics. Integration testing with Vehicle and Safety Services will validate end-to-end behavior. Field evaluation during customer evaluation period will measure actual link availability against 0.999 target.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns link availability; Vehicle owns safe operation during link transitions; Safety Services owns safe-state logic if link is unavailable. Responsibilities are non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Document the Network Link interface contract (latency, packet loss, timeout thresholds) to ensure Teleoperation can reliably detect path degradation.
Define failover latency requirement (e.g., < 100 ms) and validate that DP-B can meet it.
Specify health monitoring frequency and thresholds (e.g., heartbeat interval, consecutive missed heartbeats before failover).
Clarify that network infrastructure provisioning (selecting primary/backup providers) is a separate concern, not owned by Teleoperation.
Document that Vehicle module is link-agnostic and does not implement failover logic.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command link, telemetry link, link degradation detection, redundancy/failover). DP-B is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module. However, the sub-FRs require further decomposition to define detailed failover logic, health monitoring algorithms, and interface contracts. Proceeding to the next level will refine the design and identify implementation details (e.g., heartbeat protocol, failover state machine, latency budgets).
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss and report status to Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to minimize link downtime.

assumptions: Two independent network paths are available at the worksite (customer responsibility).
Failover latency < 100 ms is acceptable for safe teleoperation (to be validated).
Network Link interface abstraction is feasible and will be provided by network infrastructure layer.
Vehicle module will remain link-agnostic and only consume Tasking/produce Telemetry.
Safety Services will consume SafetyAlert and implement safe-state logic without controlling failover.
Evaluation period is defined by customer (e.g., 30 days, 90 days) and is sufficient to measure 0.999 availability.
open_risks: Risk: Both network paths fail simultaneously; mitigation requires network provider redundancy (out of scope).
Risk: Failover latency exceeds acceptable threshold; mitigation requires optimization of health monitoring and failover logic.
Risk: Health monitoring overhead consumes excessive bandwidth; mitigation requires efficient heartbeat protocol design.
Risk: Operator experience degradation during failover; mitigation requires operator training and UI feedback.
Risk: Network infrastructure provisioning is delayed or incomplete; mitigation requires early coordination with network providers.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification (latency, packet loss, timeout thresholds).
Failover latency requirement and acceptance criteria.
Health monitoring frequency and thresholds (heartbeat interval, consecutive missed heartbeats).
Network infrastructure provisioning plan (primary/backup providers, geographic diversity, independence criteria).
Vehicle module interface specification (Tasking input, Telemetry output) to ensure link-agnostic design.
Safety Services interface specification (SafetyAlert input) to ensure unidirectional coupling.
Field evaluation plan and measurement methodology for link availability.
